title: The Decline of Liberalism in an Era of Autopsies and Prophecies
content:
The world has entered an era of autopsies and prophecies. Capitalism inarguably produced great wealth, even as it often engendered extraordinary violence. Between the Napoleonic Wars and recent trade wars, liberal values of multiculturalism and cosmopolitanism have come into question. The rise of China has made globalization a controversial topic, and populist nationalist governments around the world have been steadily eroding past democratic gains. Formerly the victorious ideology of the twentieth century, liberalism now appears to be losing.

In recent years, many critics have converged on the idea that liberalism is a lost cause. Some insist that capitalism seeks to commodify ever more of the world and of human experience. This defining feature of the system ensures that the arc of capitalist history often bends toward the degradation of dignity. No matter the many ways people have resisted the spread of capitalist power, their victories were always fleeting. The logic of profit often sought to justify coercion as liberation.

In various parts of the world, European powers advanced campaigns to push societies to embrace unregulated markets. In this narrative, liberalism created a pressure release valve for an exploitative system. It offered the illusion of inclusion while capitalism concentrated power and wealth among a rarefied elite. It's hard to dispute the record, as many capitalists have had skittish commitments to liberal values. Throughout history, big businesses have sometimes accommodated dictatorships when it suited their financial interests.

However, this view collides with the conventional narrative of capitalism's fundamental bond with liberty. That notion holds that liberalism transformed capitalism. The belief in the rights of the individual freed people from previously oppressive governments. This creed constrained the actions of the state and gradually tamed capitalism. The most famous defenders of this optimistic position argue that economic freedom is inextricably linked to political freedom.
